在疫情爆发期间，旧金山许多试图依靠外卖服务生存的餐厅也一直在悄悄应对一个意想不到的财务障碍：DoorDash收取30%的佣金，尽管该市在4月份将外卖应用收取的佣金上限定在15%。
旧金山厨师克里斯蒂安·西斯克(Christian Ciscle)本周在社交媒体上引起了人们对这笔费用的关注。西斯科在旧金山教会区经营着一家颇受欢迎的炸鸡店。他在推特上发布了DoorDash的一条消息的截图，显示佣金率为30%。
DoorDash后来就这条推文联系了Ciscle，告诉他这条推文并没有反映出他实际上被收取的较低佣金费率。但DoorDash的一名代表告诉“纪事报”，旧金山还有其他餐厅，在该公司与之合作的数千家餐厅中，只有不到10家实际上错误地收取了30%的佣金。该公司表示，将补偿错误收取的费用。
DoorDash在给“纪事报”的一封电子邮件中说：“我们对这种情况造成的任何混乱表示遗憾，并正在积极努力，让我们的商业合作伙伴更清楚地了解我们目前的佣金结构。”
对于Ciscle来说，当涉及到与送货应用程序相关的费用时，他在公司的经历代表着旧金山餐厅的一个更大的问题。
SF Chickenbox的所有者西斯克告诉“纪事报”：“在COVID之前，餐馆不可能靠所有这些应用程序和它们的费用生存下去。”“如果你花25%到30%购买一款应用程序，你就不可能生存下来，甚至不可能出人头地。”
今年早些时候，监管人员亚伦·佩斯金(Aaron Peskin)和艾莎·萨法伊(Ahsha Safaí)发起谈判，限制优步(Uber Eats)、GrubHub、DoorDash和Postmate等送餐公司的佣金，作为对就地避难所订单对餐厅财务影响的紧急回应。当伦敦布赖德市长在4月份宣布这项立法时，旧金山成为加州第一个代表餐馆采取此类行动的城市。这项立法要求被发现违反上限的第三方应用程序向通知他们多收费用的餐厅提供退款。
今年6月，GrubHub曾短暂开始向一些当地餐厅收取超过15%的费用。格鲁布中心在一份声明中告诉SFGate，在旧金山最初的上限到期后，费用上调了。
作为回应，市政府官员修改了上限的措辞，以澄清，当修改避难所就地令以允许在旧金山室内就餐时，上限将终止。
这可能需要一段时间。作为当地重新开业计划的一部分，旧金山的餐厅在6月份获准提供户外用餐服务。从7月13日开始，室内用餐将被允许。该计划于周二被取消，因为数据显示病毒感染正在加州各地爆炸式增长。
根据国家数据，在旧金山湾区，截至周二，与新冠肺炎一起住院的人数攀升至504人。孔特拉科斯塔、马林、纳帕、圣马特奥、圣克拉拉和索诺马县都报告了单日住院人数增加。
“我们还没有走出困境，”布赖德在周二的新闻发布会上说。
在大流行期间，送货应用市场对旧金山餐厅的重要性与日俱增。根据最近的数据，即使在下单之前，美国就有大约4000万第三方送货应用程序用户。在过去的几个月里，这个数字有所增长。但是，尽管这些服务在顾客中很受欢迎，旧金山的厨师们长期以来一直在抱怨与DoorDash做生意的成本。
这种情况刺激了当地餐馆之间的演变。一些人放弃了这些应用程序，转而创建自己的内部送货服务业务，就像旧金山任务区的Prairie所做的那样。
草原主人安东尼·斯特朗最近告诉“纪事报”：“对我们所有人来说，拥有自己的东西是一个更好的选择。”
更新：这个故事已经更新，澄清了Christian Ciscle与DoorDash互动的方方面面。他收到DoorDash发来的一条信息，指出送货佣金为30%。
note_en:
Numerous San Francisco restaurants trying to survive on takeout service during the pandemic have also quietly been dealing with an unexpected financial hurdle: 30% commission fees from DoorDash, despite the fact that  the city capped fees charged by food delivery apps at 15% in April.
  San Francisco chef Christian Ciscle, who runs a popular fried chicken spot in San Francisco’s Mission District, drew attention to the fees  on social media this week. He posted a screenshot on Twitter of a message from DoorDash that showed a 30% commission rate.
  DoorDash later contacted Ciscle about the tweet, and told him it did not reflect the lower commission rate he was actually being charged. But a DoorDash representative told The Chronicle there were other restaurants in San Francisco, fewer than 10 out of the thousands the company works with in the city, that were actually mistakenly being charged 30% commission rates. The company said it will reimburse the incorrectly charged fees.
  “We regret any confusion caused in this instance and are actively working to make the communication around our current commission structure clearer for our merchant partners,” DoorDash said in an email to The Chronicle.
  For Ciscle, his experience with the company represents a larger issue for San Francisco restaurants when it comes to the fees tied to delivery apps.
  “It was impossible for restaurants before COVID to survive with all of these apps and their fees,” Ciscle, owner of SF Chickenbox, told The Chronicle. “If you’re giving 25% to 30% to an app, there’s no way you’re going to survive, or even get ahead.”
     Supervisors Aaron Peskin and Ahsha Safaí initiated talks to cap commission fees from food delivery companies like Uber Eats, Grubhub, DoorDash and Postmates earlier this year as an emergency response to the financial impact of shelter-in-place orders on restaurants. When Mayor London Breed announced the legislation in April, San Francisco became the first city in California to take such action  on behalf of restaurants. The legislation requires third-party apps found to be in violation of the cap to provide refunds to restaurants who notify them they were overcharged.
  In June, Grubhub briefly  began charging some local restaurants more than 15% in fees. The hike in fees followed the expiration of San Francisco’s initial cap, Grubhub told SFGate in a statement.
  In response,  city officials amended the language of the cap to clarify that it will terminate when the shelter-in-place order is amended to allow indoor dining in San Francisco.
  That may take a while. San Francisco restaurants were allowed to offer outdoor meal service in June as part of the local reopening plan. Indoor dining was due to be allowed beginning July 13. That  plan was nixed Tuesday as data showed viral infections were exploding across California.
  In the Bay Area, the number of people hospitalized with COVID-19 climbed to 504 patients as of Tuesday, according to state data. Contra Costa, Marin, Napa, San Mateo, Santa Clara and Sonoma counties all reported single-day hospitalization increases.
     “We are not out of the woods yet,” Breed said during a news conference on Tuesday.
  The delivery-app market has grown in importance to San Francisco restaurants during pandemic. Even before shelter-in-place orders, there were around 40 million third-party delivery app users in the U.S., according to recent data. The numbers have grown over the past few months. But while the services are popular among customers, San Francisco  chefs have long bemoaned the cost of doing business with the DoorDashes of the world.
  The situation has spurred an evolution among local restaurants. Some have dropped the apps in favor of creating their own in-house delivery service operations, as Prairie in San Francisco’s Mission District has done.
  “Having our own thing was just a better option for us all around,” Prairie owner Anthony Strong recently told The Chronicle.
  Update: This story has been updated to clarify aspects of Christian Ciscle’s interaction with DoorDash. He received a message from DoorDash noting a 30% commission rate on deliveries.
title: DoorDash违反了SF对餐厅送货费的上限，承诺退款
title_en: DoorDash violated SF’s cap on restaurant delivery fees, promises refunds

note:
由加州大学洛杉矶分校(UCLA)材料科学家领导的一个研究小组已经展示了制造超白涂料的方法，这种涂料可以反射高达98%的太阳传入热量。这一进展展示了设计油漆的实用途径，如果在屋顶和建筑的其他部分使用这些油漆，可以显著降低冷却成本，这超出了标准的白色凉爽屋顶油漆所能达到的效果。发表在“焦耳”(Joule)杂志网站上的这一发现，是通过白天被动辐射来保持建筑物降温的重要而实用的一步。被动辐射是一种自发过程，表面反射阳光，将热量辐射到空间，冷却到潜在的亚环境温度。这可以降低室内温度，并有助于减少空调的使用和相关的二氧化碳排放。
加州大学洛杉矶分校萨缪利工程学院(UCLA Samueli School Of Engineering)材料科学与工程学助理教授、这项研究的首席研究员阿斯瓦思·拉曼(Aaswath Raman)说，在炎热的阳光灿烂的日子里，当你穿着白色T恤时，你会感觉比穿深色T恤更凉爽--这是因为白色衬衫能反射更多阳光，同样的概念也适用于建筑。阿斯瓦思·拉曼是加州大学洛杉矶分校萨缪利工程学院(UCLA Samueli School Of Engineering)材料科学与工程学助理教授，也是这项研究的主要研究员。粉刷成白色的屋顶内部会比深色的屋顶凉爽。但这些颜料还能做别的事情：它们能阻挡红外线波长的热量，而这是我们人类用肉眼看不见的。这可以让建筑物通过辐射冷却进一步降温。
目前市场上性能最好的白色涂料通常能反射大约85%的入射太阳辐射。其余的被化学成分吸收。研究人员表明，对油漆成分进行简单的修改就可以提供显著的跳跃，反射高达98%的入射辐射。
目前的白色涂料具有很高的太阳反射率，使用的是氧化钛。虽然该化合物对大多数可见光和近红外光的反射率很高，但它也能吸收紫外线和紫外光。这种化合物的紫外线吸收特性使其在防晒霜中很有用，但它们也会导致阳光下的加热-这阻碍了尽可能保持凉爽。
研究人员研究了用廉价和容易获得的成分取代氧化钛，如重晶石，这是一种艺术家的颜料，以及动力聚四氟乙烯，更为人所知的是特氟龙。这些成分可以帮助油漆反射紫外线。该团队还对涂料的配方进行了进一步的改进，包括降低聚合物粘合剂的浓度，聚合物粘合剂也会吸收热量。
加州大学洛杉矶分校(UCLA)博士后学者乔奥蒂莫伊·曼达尔(Jyotirmoy Mandal)说，这样做可能会在不久的将来实现潜在的降温效益，因为我们提出的修改方案在油漆和涂料行业的能力范围内。他是施密特科学公司(Schmidt Science)的研究员，也是这项研究的共同通讯作者。
除了这一进展，作者们还提出了进一步研究的几个长期影响，包括绘制这种颜料可以发挥作用的地方的地图，研究污染对技术的影响，以及在全球范围内，如果它们能够削弱地球自身反射太阳热量的能力的话。
研究人员还指出，包括加利福尼亚州和纽约市在内的许多市政当局和政府已经开始鼓励新建筑的凉爽屋顶技术。
研究冷却涂料技术多年的曼达尔说，我们希望这项工作将刺激未来在超白涂料方面的倡议，不仅可以节省建筑能源，还可以减轻城市的热岛效应，甚至可能展示一种实用的方式，如果大规模应用，可能会影响气候变化。曼达尔研究冷却涂料技术已有数年。这将需要光学、材料科学和气象学等不同领域的专家以及来自行业和政策部门的专家之间的合作。更多信息：Jyotirmoy Mandal等人，Joule(2020)，Paints as a Scalable and Efficient Radiative Cooling Technology for Building，Joule(2020)。doi：10.1016/j.joule.2020.04.010
note_en:
A research team led by UCLA materials scientists has demonstrated ways to make super white paint that reflects as much as 98% of incoming heat from the sun. The advance shows practical pathways for designing paints that, if used on rooftops and other parts of a building, could significantly reduce cooling costs, beyond what standard white 'cool-roof' paints can achieve.    The findings, published online in  Joule, are a major and practical step towards keeping buildings cooler by passive daytime radiative  —a spontaneous process in which a surface reflects sunlight and radiates heat into space, cooling down to potentially sub-ambient temperatures. This can lower indoor temperatures and help cut down on air conditioner use and associated carbon dioxide emissions.
  "When you wear a white T-shirt on a hot sunny day, you feel cooler than if you wore one that's darker in color—that's because the white shirt reflects more sunlight and it's the same concept for buildings," said Aaswath Raman, an assistant professor of materials science and engineering at UCLA Samueli School of Engineering, and the principal investigator on the study. "A roof painted white will be cooler inside than one in a darker shade. But those paints also do something else: they reject heat at infrared wavelengths, which we humans cannot see with our eyes. This could allow buildings to cool down even more by radiative cooling."
  The best performing white paints currently available typically reflect around 85% of incoming solar radiation. The remainder is absorbed by the chemical makeup of the  . The researchers showed that simple modifications in a paint's ingredients could offer a significant jump, reflecting as much as 98% of incoming radiation.
  Current white paints with high solar reflectance use titanium oxide. While the compound is very reflective of most visible and near-infrared light, it also absorbs ultraviolet and violet light. The compound's UV absorption qualities make it useful in sunscreen lotions, but they also lead to heating under sunlight—which gets in the way of keeping a   as cool as possible.
  The researchers examined replacing titanium oxide with inexpensive and readily available ingredients such as barite, which is an artist's pigment, and powered polytetrafluoroethylene, better known as Teflon. These ingredients help paints reflect UV light. The team also made further refinements to the paint's formula, including reducing the concentration of polymer binders, which also absorb heat.
  "The potential cooling benefits this can yield may be realized in the near future because the modifications we propose are within the capabilities of the paint and coatings industry," said UCLA postdoctoral scholar Jyotirmoy Mandal, a Schmidt Science Fellow working in Raman's research group and the co-corresponding author on the research.
  Beyond the advance, the authors suggested several long-term implications for further study, including mapping where such paints could make a difference, studying the effect of pollution on   technologies, and on a global scale, if they could make a dent on the earth's own ability to reflect heat from the sun.
  The researchers also noted that many municipalities and governments, including the state of California and New York City, have started to encourage cool-roof technologies for new buildings.
  "We hope that the work will spur future initiatives in super-white coatings for not only energy savings in buildings, but also mitigating the heat island effects of cities, and perhaps even showing a practical way that, if applied on a massive, global scale could affect climate change," said Mandal, who has studied cooling paint technologies for several years. "This would require a collaboration among experts in diverse fields like optics, materials science and meteorology, and experts from the industry and policy sectors."        More information:												Jyotirmoy Mandal et al, Paints as a Scalable and Effective Radiative Cooling Technology for Buildings,  Joule (2020).  DOI: 10.1016/j.joule.2020.04.010
title: 用超白涂料使建筑物更凉爽
title_en: Keeping buildings cooler with super-white paints

note:
谷歌本周发布了ipad gmail应用程序的更新，支持分屏多任务处理，你可以随意将其归入“迟做总比不做好”一栏，但这已经太晚了，以至于近乎荒唐。这就好比时髦地迟到和聚会一周后才出现之间的区别。早在2015年，iOS9就为iPad引入了分屏多任务处理。五年后，它增加了对iPad用户体验基础元素的支持。在人们想要使用分屏的应用程序类型列表中，电子邮件客户端几乎位居榜首。五年了。
谷歌为品味不佳的用户制作了很多用户体验糟糕的软件。特别是，他们的iOS软件在很大程度上从未表明它是由喜欢 - 甚至使用 - iOS的人设计的。是盲人在引导盲人。但Gmail应用程序目前在App Store的生产力类别中是排名第一的免费应用程序。
从表面上看，把这件事抛诸脑后是很有诱惑力的。各自为政。不管是什么能让他们的船浮起来。谁会在乎数百万iPad用户对使用一个糟糕的iPad应用程序的电子邮件客户端是否满意，只要那些真正关心的人可以使用真正好的iPad电子邮件客户端呢？
但是，那些被困在使用Gmail应用程序的人怎么办，不是因为他们想要，而是因为他们不得不这样做？除了苹果，还有谁能帮助他们呢？
我担心，从长远来看，指望苹果继续努力创造制作精良的 - 是站不住脚的，更不用说疯狂的伟大的 - 软件了，因为这么多用户不仅满足于，甚至可能更喜欢软件，说得好听些，就是垃圾软件。一直都有一些流行的mac和iphone应用程序客观上是糟糕的应用程序 - ，我所说的“流行”指的是大量使用，而不是很受欢迎。但苹果用户之所以苹果用户，是因为如果他们不得不使用一个糟糕的应用程序，他们会大声抱怨。Word 6是微软扔在Mac用户门廊上的一袋狗屎，但我们都知道它是一袋燃烧的狗屎，即使是我们这些甚至不使用Word的人也对此感到愤怒，因为这是一种侮辱。
我担心这种“谁在乎，总比什么都没有强”的态度已经渗透到苹果本身，并解释了我们是如何最终在Mac上将几乎没有修改的iPad应用作为系统应用发货的。
但最让我担心的是，这体现了苹果在App Store上迷失了方向。如果首先不是为了确保它们是好的应用程序，那么对应用程序运行严格的审批流程到底有什么意义？一款五年内不支持分屏多任务处理的iPad电子邮件应用程序，从定义上讲，不是一款好的应用程序。
我希望看到苹果应用的所有活力、活力和警觉，以确保应用商店上的任何应用都不会赚到一分钱，而苹果没有得到3美分的应用，以确保没有任何欺诈或欺诈，并且在操作系统引入平台良好公民功能后，流行应用在合理的时间内支持这些功能。我不知道“合理”到底有多长，但分屏支持他妈的五年不是。
你可能会争辩说，App Store中有一百万个应用程序，苹果不能确保每一个应用程序在质量上都达到标准。但是，没有必要仔细检查一百万个应用程序， - 只需从拥有一百万用户的应用程序开始。一款应用程序越受欢迎，苹果就越应该从它是该平台的体面公民的角度来审视它。如果他们打算严格审查App Store，他们就应该以用户体验的名义严格审查。
iPad上安装量最大的生产力应用被允许在没有支持分屏这样对平台至关重要的东西的情况下停滞不前五年，这与嘿的纠葛一样，是对App Store现状的谴责。这是同一枚硬币的另一面。
App Store的主要目的应该是引导第三方应用程序走向卓越，使平台作为一个整体尽可能地出色。当史蒂夫·乔布斯在2008年介绍App Store时，他说：“我们不打算从App Store中赚钱。我们基本上把所有的钱都给了开发商，还给了30%的店铺运营费，那就太棒了。“。真的。现在的App Store似乎最优先考虑的是在苹果季度财务电子表格的服务栏中创造不断增长的营收，这种心态是不可能与之相适应的，因为现在的App Store似乎最优先考虑的是如何在苹果季度财务电子表格的服务栏中创造出不断增长的收入。
无可否认，苹果拥有强大的实力，因为App Store是iPhone和iPad所有消费软件的独家来源。为什么不以用户体验的名义使用这种能力呢？想象一下，如果开发者在提交一款应用程序进行审查时，最大的担忧不是他们是否向苹果提供了足够的收入分成，而是他们是否为用户提供了足够好的原生平台体验。不支持画中画的视频应用？你被赶出了商店。应用程序不支持动态字体大小，但显然应该支持？你出局了。辅助功能支持差吗？出去。不支持分屏的流行电子邮件客户端？出去。
与其眼睁睁地看着苹果面对美国和欧洲的反垄断监管机构，我会满怀喜悦地看着。“这家公司正在滥用其市场主导地位，从我们的资金中分得不公平的份额”，这是向政府监管机构提出的一个由来已久的抱怨。“这家公司正在滥用其市场主导地位，迫使我们让我们的应用程序更好地为用户服务”将是令人愉快的新领域。只有苹果才能做到这一点。
好的产品通常(但不幸的是，并不总是)能产生利润。成功地驾驭这款充满活力的 - ，创造出人们想要购买的始终如一的伟大产品，赚取利润是自然的副产品，简而言之，这就是苹果整个40年的历史。但是，追求利润，作为其自身的目的，并不会产生卓越的 - ，实际上通常会产生相反的结果。像任何一家伟大的公司一样，苹果理所当然地受到永不满足的胃口的推动，但这种胃口应该是为世界增加更多的艺术和技术卓越，而不是从世界上挖掘更多的钱。
你不能在试图榨取每一分钱的同时，把每一分欢乐、每一分美丽、每一分优雅都塞进一件东西里。
你可以合理地辩称，创造收入不是当今应用商店的最高优先事项，但你不能严肃地辩称，它不是最优先的 - ，仅此一项就与乔布斯的创始描述相冲突。--↩︎
note_en:
Feel free to file Google’s release this week of  an update to their iPad Gmail app with support for split-screen multitasking under “better late than never”, but this is  so late it borders on the absurd. It’s like the difference between showing up fashionably late and showing up a week after the party. Split-screen multitasking  was introduced for the iPad back in 2015 with iOS 9.  Five years to add support for a foundational element of the iPad user experience. And an email client is near the top of the list of the type of apps where someone would want to use split-screen. Five years.
 Google makes a lot of software with terrible user experiences for users who have poor taste. Their iOS software, in particular, has for the most part never suggested that it was designed by people who like — or even use — iOS. It’s the blind leading the blind. But yet the Gmail app is currently  the number one free app in the Productivity category in the App Store.
 On the surface, it’s tempting to blow this off.  To each their own. Whatever floats their boat. Who cares if millions of iPad users are satisfied using an email client that is a poor iPad app, so long as  actual  good  iPad  email clients are available to those who do care?
 But what about those stuck using the Gmail app not because they want to,  but because they  have to? Who can help them but Apple?
 I worry that it’s not tenable in the long run to expect Apple to continue striving to create well-crafted — let alone insanely great — software when so many of its users not only settle for, but perhaps even prefer, software that is, to put it kindly, garbage. There have always been popular Mac and iPhone apps that are objectively terrible apps — where by “popular” I mean much-used, not much-loved.  But what made Apple users Apple users is that they complained vociferously if they had to use a terrible app.  Word 6 was a sack of dog shit Microsoft dropped off and set aflame on Mac users’ porch, but we all knew it was a flaming bag of dog shit, and even those of us who didn’t even use Word were angry about it because it was an insult.
 I worry that this sort of “ Who cares, it’s better than nothing” attitude has seeped into Apple itself, and explains  how we wound up with barely modified  iPad apps shipping as system apps on the Mac.
 But more than anything I worry that this exemplifies where Apple has lost its way with the App Store. What exactly is the point of running a strict approval process for apps if not, first and foremost, to ensure that they’re good apps? An iPad email app that doesn’t support split-screen multitasking for five years is, by definition, not a good app.
 I’d like to see all the vim, vigor, and vigilance Apple applies to making sure no app on the App Store is making a dime without Apple getting three cents applied instead to making sure there aren’t any  scams or ripoffs, and that popular apps support good-citizen-of-the-platform features within a reasonable amount of time after those features are introduced in the OS. I don’t know exactly how long “reasonable” is, but five fucking years for split-screen support ain’t it.
 You might argue that there are a million apps in the App Store and Apple can’t make sure every one of them is up to snuff quality-wise. But there’s no need to scrutinize a million apps — just start with the apps with a million users. The more popular an app is the more Apple should scrutinize it in terms of being, simply, a decent citizen of the platform. If they’re going to be stringent about App Store review, they should be stringent in the name of user experience.
 That the iPad’s most-installed productivity app was allowed to languish for half a decade without supporting something as fundamental to the platform as split-screen is every bit as much a condemnation of the state of the App Store as  the Hey imbroglio was. It’s the other side of the same coin.
 The primary purpose of the App Store should be to steer third-party apps toward excellence, to make the platform as a whole as insanely great as possible.  When Steve Jobs introduced the App Store in 2008, he said, “We don’t intend to make any money off the App Store. We’re basically giving all the money to the developers and the 30 percent that pays for running the store, that’ll be great.” Really. It’s impossible to square that mindset with the App Store of today, where the highest priority  1 seemingly is the generation of ever-increasing revenue in the Services column of Apple’s quarterly finance spreadsheet.
 Apple undeniably wields great power from the fact that the App Store is the exclusive source for all consumer software for the iPhone and iPad. Why not use that power in the name of user experience? Imagine a world where the biggest fear developers had when submitting an app for review wasn’t whether they were offering Apple a sufficient cut of their revenue, but whether they were offering users a good enough native-to-the-platform experience. Video app that doesn’t support picture-in-picture? You’re out of the store. App doesn’t support dynamic type size but clearly should? You’re out. Poor accessibility support? Out. Popular email client that doesn’t support split screen? Out.
 Rather than watch Apple face antitrust regulators in the U.S. and Europe with a sense of dread, I’d watch with a sense of glee. “ This company is abusing its market dominance to take an unfair share of our money” is an age-old complaint to government regulators. “ This company is abusing its market dominance to force us to make our apps better for users” would be delightful new territory. Only Apple could do that.
 Great products often (but, sadly, not always) generate profit. Successfully navigating this dynamic — earning profits as a natural byproduct of the creation of consistently great products that people want to buy — is the story of Apple’s entire 40-year history in a nutshell. But profit seeking, as an end unto itself, does not generate excellence — and in fact generally results in the opposite. Apple, like any great company, is rightfully driven by an insatiable appetite, but that appetite ought to be for adding ever more artistic and technical excellence to the world, not mining ever more money from it.
 You can’t pack every last ounce of joy, beauty, and elegance  into something while simultaneously trying to squeeze every last dollar  out of it.
 You can reasonably argue that revenue generation is not  the highest priority of today’s App Store, but you can’t seriously argue that it isn’t  a top priority — and that alone puts it in conflict with Jobs’s founding description.  ↩︎
title: 关于App Store的理由的一个清晰的时刻
title_en: A Moment of Clarity Regarding the Raison D’Etre for the App Store

note:
印度军方要求其人员从他们的智能手机上删除89个应用程序，包括Facebook、TikTok、Truecaller和Instagram，以堵住信息泄露。
在与中国的紧张局势不断升级，以及对数据隐私的担忧之际，印度军方周三要求其人员从手机上删除89个应用程序。
军方发布命令，要求人员从手机上删除脸书、Truecaller、Instagram等热门应用程序和绝地求生等游戏。
这一最新指示是为了堵住武装部队人员手机中敏感国家安全信息的泄露。
这些人员还被要求删除Tinder和Hinger等约会应用程序和寄宿应用Couchsurfing，以及Daily Hunt等新闻应用程序。
军方已将7月15日定为安全部队人员从手机上删除89个应用程序的最后期限。
然而，只要他们不在这些平台上透露自己的军人背景，他们就可以使用WhatsApp、Telegram、Signal、YouTube和Twitter等社交媒体和即时通讯应用。
note_en:
The Indian Army has asked its personnel to delete 89 apps from their smartphones including Facebook, TikTok, Truecaller and Instagram to plug leakage of information
  Amid rising tensions with China and concerns over data privacy, the Indian Army on Wednesday asked its personnel to delete 89 apps from their phones.
 Issuing an order, the Army asked personnel to delete popular apps such as Facebook, Truecaller, Instagram and games like PUBG from their mobile phones.
 The latest instruction comes as a bid to plug leakage of sensitive national security information from phones of armed forces personnel.
  The personnel have also been asked to delete dating apps such as Tinder and Hinge and homestay app Couchsurfing as well as news apps like Daily Hunt.
 The Army has set July 15 as the deadline for the security forces personnel to remove the 89 apps from their phones.
 However, they will be allowed to use social media and messaging apps like WhatsApp, Telegram, Signal, YouTube and Twitter as long as they don't reveal their army background on the platforms.
title: 印度陆军人员被禁止使用89款应用程序
title_en: Indian Army Personnel Banned From Using 89 Apps

note:
我的书“基于证据的软件工程：基于公开可用的数据”现已发布测试版(pdf和代码+数据)。计划进行三个月的审查，最终版本将在圣诞节前在商店里买到(我计划打印几百本，并在亚马逊上提供)。
接下来的几个月将用来回应读者的评论，并从我等待分析的剩余20多个数据集添加材料。
你可以给我发电子邮件发表任何评论，也可以在这本书的Github页面上添加一期。
虽然内容与我最初的想法有很大的不同，但10年前，讨论所有公开可用的软件工程数据的最初目的已经实现了(在某些情况下，更详细的数据，数量更多，已经取代了早期不太详细/更小的数据集)。
如果有公共数据，只讨论一个主题的目的在某些地方略有偏差(因为我认为数据会出现，但它没有出现，或者我想连接两个数据集，或者我还没有删除已经写好的内容)。
这两个目的的结果是，讨论的流程是非常脱节的，甚至是脱节的。另一个原因可能是我还没有想出如何以一种明智的方式将这些材料联系起来。我是第一个通过这个练习的人，所以我不知道它会去哪里。
大约620多个数据集比我认为可以公开获得的大三到四倍。更多的数据是好消息，但需要更多的时间来分析和讨论。
note_en:
My book,  Evidence-based software engineering: based on the publicly available data is now out on beta release ( pdf, and  code+data). The plan is for a three-month review, with the final version available in the shops in time for Christmas (I plan to get a few hundred printed, and made available on Amazon).
 The next few months will be spent responding to reader comments, and adding material from the remaining 20 odd datasets I have waiting to be analysed.
 You can either email me with any comments, or add an issue to the  book’s Github page.
 While the content is very different from my original thoughts, 10-years ago, the original aim of discussing all the publicly available software engineering data has been carried through (in some cases more detailed data, in greater quantity, has supplanted earlier less detailed/smaller datasets).
 The aim of only discussing a topic if public data is available, has been slightly bent in places (because I thought data would turn up, and it didn’t, or I wanted to connect two datasets, or I have not yet deleted what has been written).
 The outcome of these two aims is that the flow of discussion is very disjoint, even disconnected. Another reason might be that I have not yet figured out how to connect the material in a sensible way. I’m the first person to go through this exercise, so I have no idea where it’s going.
 The roughly 620+ datasets is three to four times larger than I thought was publicly available. More data is good news, but required more time to analyse and discuss.
title: 基于证据的软件工程-测试版图书

note:
评论微软在.NET平台上工作的主要人员对它与一家公司捆绑在一起的持续看法感到担忧。
微软软件架构师大卫·福勒(David Fowler)说，告诉你的朋友，C#和F#是免费的、开源的，可以在Linux和Mac上运行。福勒从事.NET Core和ASP.NET的设计，也是SignalR实时消息传递框架的共同发明人。
福勒在周末发表了这番言论，这是.NET开发人员比利·柯林斯在推特上引发的一场辩论的一部分。他说：非.NET开发人员认为C#是一种仅限Windows的企业膨胀软件语言，这是怎么回事？现在已经不是2005年了。
问题是，尽管微软在2014年开始开源.NET，但有一种看法认为，它仍然是一个微软平台框架，阻碍了更广泛生态系统的增长。
在这方面很难获得可靠的数据。根据StackOverflow的调查，.NET的主要语言C#仍然很受欢迎，有31.4%的开发人员使用，在RedMonk语言排行榜上排名第五。根据RedMonk的数据，自2012年以来，C#的使用量一直保持稳定。
也就是说，微软的平台有一个庞大的社区，这些数据与.NET Core的演变成功地留住了现有的开发人员，但不太善于吸引新的开发人员的想法是一致的。今年1月，该社区的一些成员反思了如下问题：目前的.NET是为40岁的白人男性准备的，而.NET并没有得到更年轻的开发人员的太多关爱。
微软已经证明了自己有能力创建拥有广泛用户基础的开源项目，两个例子是Visual Studio代码程序员的编辑器和打字语言(由C#的发明者Anders Hejlsberg创建)。两者都广泛用于非Windows计算机，以及与Microsoft无关的项目中。
每个人都认为StackOverflow是.NET构建的最佳系统的唯一真正的例子。还需要更多的例子。&#34；
生态系统仍然远远落后于其他语言，并且仍然非常依赖微软…。就Java而言，Spring Boot背后有一大群公司，但.NET Core的情况就不一样了，因为它主要由微软支持。
福勒倾向于认为这是一个可以修复的感知问题。他评论说：我们跨平台已经有5年了，在此之前，微软从未认可过Mono(Linux版.NET的第三方实现)。一些障碍是技术性的，我们已经在2020年消除了大部分障碍。现在我们的生态系统正在迎头赶上。
虽然微软确实很难在自己的平台之外传播.NET的价值，但也有可能该公司未能在开放技术和保留其商业优势之间找到正确的平衡。
微软有充分的商业理由让.NET跨平台。考虑到Linux在服务器上的主导地位及其在Kubernetes等平台中的角色，在Linux上良好运行至关重要。
然而，微软也使用.NET Core和Web框架ASP.NET Core来引导开发人员使用它的其他产品，不仅是Azure云，还有各种形式的SQL Server数据库管理器。微软为监督开源项目而设立的.NET基金会不可避免地向公司倾斜；尽管其他公司参与了它的治理，但它不能成为一个中立的管家。
F#的发明者唐·赛姆(Don Syme)说：[这]是我们改变的观念，不是吗？为什么不让更多的公司显眼地加入到.NET领导力的行列中来呢？使.NET成为一个合适的非军事区联盟，以反映它所涉及的巨大的经济利益。
Syme正在支持.NET核心主要项目经理Richard Lander的一项倡议，该倡议旨在提高社区贡献的信用，提高非微软贡献者的形象，并促进多样性和包容性。Syme说，规则中不应该对.NET团队或微软团队有特别不同的对待-微软的贡献者和其他人一样，只是社区的一部分。(#34；Syme#34；Syme；&#34；Syme；&#34；Syme说，规则中不应该对.NET团队或微软有特殊的区别-微软贡献者和其他人一样，只是社区的一部分。
这样的调整可能有助于.NET感知。微软还认识到，VS代码必须在扩大.NET使用方面发挥作用。高级项目经理菲利普·卡特(Philip Carter)加入了这场辩论，他补充道：拥有2-5年.NET经验的人比拥有5年以上.NET经验的人使用VS代码的次数要多得多(尽管工具性能不佳)。
不难发现，微软可以做些什么来使.NET成为一种更中立的技术，从而更有可能吸引更广泛的生态系统。这些想法包括放弃.NET Foundation，修改文档以消除与SQL Server和Azure的紧密联系，以及使VS代码与仅用于.NET开发的Windows Visual Studio一样好。
但是，这是否符合该公司的商业目标呢？还有一个风险是，如果微软为了更广泛的使用而与.NET保持距离，该公司的客户可能会担心它正在失去现有的坚实后盾。如果不想看到.NET使用率下降，这是该公司需要继续努力解决的问题。®。
The Register-独立于科技界的新闻和观点。情况发布的一部分
title: .NET核心：仍然是微软平台的问题
title_en: .NET Core: Still a Microsoft platform thing

note:
如果我设计它理想的家园，它会建造一座监狱。囚犯们日夜挤在一起；社交距离、频繁洗手和戴口罩都是幻想。许多囚犯年事已高，生病，容易感染。大多数监狱的运营都接近饱和；一些监狱容纳的囚犯比建造时多。病毒最大的五个聚集区都在监狱里；在俄亥俄州的马里恩县，95%的囚犯检测呈阳性。
警察为了小小的违法行为杀害黑人男女的鲜明而无可辩驳的画面引发了抗议，并引发了对美国如何对待与刑事司法系统相遇的人的更大规模的反思。甚至在乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)被杀之前，公众和政界人士就已经对监狱里不那么显眼但仍然令人震惊的死亡人数感到困扰，随着病毒的传播，囚犯们被困在那里，有效地将他们的监禁判决变成了死刑判决。我们如何阻止感染浪潮？根据许多倡导者的说法，答案很简单：释放囚犯。
一些州长对监狱和监狱中的死亡感到震惊，并担心对周围社区的风险，他们正在倾听-某种程度上，耳朵调谐到了政治责任。超过一半的州已经同意释放那些被判犯有低级罪行的人，即将结束刑期的人，或者值得同情释放的人，比如孕妇或年长、脆弱的囚犯。
“这是很有帮助的。我知道人们已经出来了，我为他们的获释而感动，“尼科尔·波特说，她是量刑项目的宣传部主任，这是一个倡导量刑改革的研究机构。“但这些都不是实质性的。我希望这一刻告诉我们的是，我们的监禁率是政治的函数--因为关于谁需要被监禁有很多问题。“。
乔治敦法学院教授、“有罪的人”一书的作者阿贝·史密斯说：“我们仅仅停留在表面上。”“即使我们释放了容易摘到的果实，”史密斯说--她称之为“非”：非严重、非暴力、非性侵犯者--“也不会有什么影响。”
为了有意义地减少美国监狱人口，减缓流行病，不仅需要削减脂肪，还需要削减肌肉，不仅要释放非暴力毒品罪犯，还要释放那些被判暴力犯罪的人。要做到这一点，无论是在实践上还是在道德上，难度都是巨大的。我们应该释放哪些犯有谋杀罪或持械抢劫罪的人？我们怎么决定呢？我们如何保证他们不会再次得罪，特别是在他们试图在近一个世纪以来最严重的经济崩溃期间重新开始自己的生活的时候？
在某种程度上，这场大流行为释放被判犯有暴力罪行的人-阻止病毒-提供了完美的理由。但与此同时，这也为他们的获释创造了可能最糟糕的条件：他们将难以找到工作；致力于帮助他们的组织将面临资金短缺；住房甚至食物可能遥不可及。福特汉姆大学(Fordham University)法学教授约翰·普法夫(John Pfaff)是“锁定：大规模监禁的真正原因和如何实现真正的改革”一书的作者，他指出，在这种经济形势下，我们可以看到犯罪率上升。“人们会说：‘鲍勃很早就出狱了，然后他就走了，犯下了这起抢劫案，’”他说。“抢劫的真正原因是鲍勃提前获释，没有工作，所以他犯了抢劫罪。但如果你是警察局长或(地区检察官)，很容易将经济驱动的犯罪转变为刑事司法改革驱动的犯罪。“。这是他的噩梦，很容易看出原因。
监狱里挤满了应该得到第二次机会的候选人-年轻时犯过严重错误的男男女女，他们的罪行更多地反映了年轻人的冲动和驾驭市中心暴力的狡猾，而不是性格。然而，在很大程度上，这些人并不是系统一直在准备释放的人。
监狱可以起到很多作用--首先阻止人们犯罪，如果他们犯罪了就惩罚他们，或者让他们改过自新，让他们恢复正常生活。美国大体上选择了惩罚性的道路，判处长达数十年的刑期，旨在减少街头犯罪。在此期间，囚犯通常不会接受使他们能够重返外部世界并建立新的稳定生活的培训或护理。对于那些希望现在释放囚犯的人来说，这是一个巨大的障碍。
安吉洛·罗宾逊(Angelo Robinson)因谋杀罪服刑20多年，他说：“监狱不会让你改过自新。”他告诉我，在很多方面，监狱生活与街头生活没有什么不同。“至于毒品、杀戮、打架，这些都是在监狱里。守卫不会进来试图帮你改变。不，他们也是带着他们的问题来的。“。任何救赎的推动力都来自内部。“你必须想要改变你的生活。而那里的很多人都不想这么做。“。
罗宾逊的监狱故事大约从晚上10点开始。1997年2月17日，他和几个朋友在辛辛那提西区的一套公寓里做生意。20岁的罗宾逊被锁在卧室里，守卫着隐藏的裂缝，这时他听到前门突然打开，尖叫声和枪声沿着大厅向卧室移动。“我只是想，我死定了，”罗宾逊回忆道。他朝卧室的门开枪，当公寓安静下来时，他发现他的朋友维罗妮卡·杰克逊(Veronica Jackson)死在走廊里。直到几天后，他才知道是他的子弹打死了她。“我感觉很糟糕，”他说。“我从她家里带走了一个人。她有孩子。我每天都在想这件事。“。
如果他同意承认过失杀人罪，检察官会给他14年的刑期。罗宾逊认为他可以证明自己是自卫的，于是选择了庭审。陪审团判定他犯有谋杀罪，判处他29年无期徒刑。
罗宾逊的故事是一个更大的故事，讲述了自1981年以来，美国囚犯人数是如何翻了两番多，把监狱变成了拥挤、不卫生的地方，只是在等待传染病的到来。自打击犯罪时代开始以来，被判犯有暴力甚至一些非暴力罪行的人被判处越来越长的刑期。这并不是因为美国人变得更加野蛮；事实上，自20世纪90年代初以来，暴力犯罪率已经下降了50%。但是，尽管街头暴力已经平息，但因杀人罪和非过失杀人罪在监狱服刑的时间增加了两倍，每七名囚犯中就有一人将被判终身监禁。新的罪犯到来了，旧的罪犯需要数年时间才能离开；监狱就像一座没有出口的大坝，今天水库正在淹没它的堤岸。
这一无情的政策可能对选民有情感上的吸引力，但却违背了基于统计数据的逻辑。“为什么我们要把一个人关上50年？”普法夫问道。他说，没有其他西方国家这样做，社会科学“毫不含糊地表明”，人们随着年龄的增长而进入和退出暴力行为。根据司法统计局(Bureau Of Justice Statistics)的数据，杀人罪逮捕的高峰期是19岁；强奸罪的逮捕高峰期是18岁。Pfaff说：“一个五六十岁的人已经不像他们20岁时那样暴力了。”
这些漫长的判决源于一种报复性的心态，但它们也标志着在大流行期间精简囚犯队伍的最聪明的方式：从年长的囚犯开始。量刑项目的执行主任马克·莫尔说：“每年我们都会把30岁以上的人关在监狱里，当然40岁或50岁以后，我们在公共安全方面得到的回报也在递减。”同时，Mauer说，由于糟糕的医疗保健，老年囚犯一年比一年病得更重，费用也更高。“随着时间的推移，防止犯罪率越来越低的成本变得非常巨大。”
当然，这些数字没有考虑到长期监禁的社会成本，失去父亲或母亲或儿子，情感压力，社区的瓦解，年轻人被关在监狱里度过余生时的绝望。几年来，罗宾逊沉浸在这种损失中，直到有一天他想，“你不能就这样坐在这里腐烂。”他决定接受教育。
在这里，他遇到了一个障碍，不仅是个人的实现，也是改过自新的障碍，这至少在理论上是监狱的基石。根据毛尔等专家的说法，通常情况下，可能为囚犯重新进入社会做好准备的课程、学徒和编程是一种稀缺资源，首先提供给获释一年内的囚犯。罗宾逊说：“我就在这里，29岁，终身监禁。”“我是名单上的最后一个。”罗宾逊决定另想办法。他偷偷溜进教室旁听。他设法获得了GED，大多数囚犯都可以参加GED考试。他自学了西班牙语和手语；他学会了识谱和弹吉他。他全身心地投入到工作中，学会了如何操作叉车，如何装饰家具，如何做饭。
毛尔说，对接近释放的囚犯进行优先排序会惩罚像罗宾逊这样的囚犯，他们有很长的刑期，但有可能获得假释。假释委员会不仅会考虑不良行为，还会考虑好的行为，不仅会考虑纪律处分，还会考虑班级和学徒制度，这表明你想要让自己变得更好。Mauer说，像罗宾逊这样的人会说，“不是因为你不在乎，而是因为你没有机会接触到程序。”“所以他们说，‘你被拒绝了，因为你没有良好的监狱记录。’”
但这一次，罗宾逊是幸运的。2012年，俄亥俄州司法与政策中心(Ohio Justice And Policy Center)执行主任大卫·辛格尔顿(David Singleton)代表他提起民权诉讼，迫使监狱允许他接受必要的手术。辛格尔顿说：“我知道有一天我会很乐意做我的邻居的。”在接下来的几年里，同时也是北肯塔基大学大通法学院教授和前公设辩护人的辛格尔顿发展了一种违反直觉的方法：说服把罪犯关进监狱的人倡导释放他。去年，俄亥俄州司法与政策中心(Ohio Justice And Policy Center)启动了一个项目，与检察官和受害者家属合作，确保那些被判犯有更严重罪行(包括暴力犯罪)的人获释。
“我们是有罪的项目，”辛格尔顿笑着说。“我们希望代表那些有罪但仍是人的人，他们不应该被抹杀。”要被考虑，囚犯必须承认自己的罪行，服刑很长一段时间，并证明有改过自新的证据，无论是通过正式的课程，还是通过在监狱中指导和成为一股向善的力量而得到声誉上的改过自新的证据。该项目还寻找那些已经不再犯罪的人：那些在十几岁和20岁出头被判有罪，但有几年时间让自己的行为变得成熟的人。
罗宾逊成为该项目的第一位客户。辛格尔顿与维罗妮卡·杰克逊的家人联系，要求支持释放他，除了一名亲属外，所有人都同意了。受害者的妹妹莎拉·杰克逊(Sarah Jackson)在一份宣誓书中写道：“这起犯罪发生时，他还是一个年轻人，他已经服刑足够长了。”“现在是他回家和家人团聚的时候了。”受害者的儿子克利福德·杰克逊(Clifford Jackson)过去曾表达过保留意见，他在2019年7月告诉“纽约时报”：“我们从未得到任何补偿。我们只能从他的时间中得到补偿。“。记者无法联系到他对这篇报道发表评论。俄亥俄州汉密尔顿县首席助理检察官史蒂夫·托尔伯特(Steve Tolbert)与辛格尔顿一起向法院请愿，要求释放罗宾逊。托尔伯特告诉我：“从道义上讲，感觉就是对的。”“这个人犯了一个巨大的错误，在这种有限的情况下，我们愿意冒这个险。”2019年8月1日，鲁滨逊承认过失杀人罪，走出监狱。他在监狱里度过了22年，大半生，但在43岁的时候，他希望自己的余生能自由度过。
去年，15名被判犯有暴力罪行的人-其中包括3名被判谋杀罪-因超越罪行而获释。辛格尔顿说，他们都“很好地适应了生活的起起落落”--在经济衰退和大流行期间，这可不是一件小事。但这个故事既令人望而生畏，也令人振奋。除非州长单方面释放大量的人-这是一个极不可能的前景-这些囚犯将是最罕见的例外。有多少检察官愿意向法院请愿，要求释放一名被判犯有暴力罪的罪犯？有多少受害者的家人会支持强奸、杀害或殴打他们妹妹的人的自由？还有多少法律机构有资金或人员来打这场艰苦、耗时的战斗？据我所知：接近于零。去年，“超越罪恶感”使俄亥俄州49,000人的监狱人口减少了0.0003%。这就像试图用茶匙清空大海一样。
在大流行期间，谁能幸存下来，建立稳定的生活似乎几乎是不可能的。这在正常情况下已经够难的了。米歇尔·罗宾逊(Michelle Robinson)很清楚这一点。
2007年4月4日晚，罗宾逊(与安吉洛没有亲属关系)允许两名男性熟人
回归平民生活，特别是对于那些在监狱里度过了十年或更长时间的人来说，可能是一段令人迷失方向的艰难攀登。找公寓，找到工作，申请驾照，在你每小时挣40美分的情况下还清子女抚养费等债务-所有这些都使过渡成为一项西西弗式的任务。州和地方政府没有义务帮助囚犯重新进入社会。“他们不需要做任何事情，”莫尔指出，“而且在大多数情况下，他们的工作做得非常不充分。”
一些城市，如纽约，提供一系列计划-工作培训和安置，经济适用房，药物治疗计划-通常是通过资助非营利性社区或宗教组织。但是，如果你不幸来自阿拉巴马州或密西西比州的农村-事实上几乎任何农村社区-你基本上只能靠自己了。
大流行加剧了问题。这些已经在善意和小本经营下运作的组织，能否一次处理成百上千名被释放的人呢？“这会把(他们)拉到极限，”辛格尔顿说。
“每个州都将经历一场金融危机，”中右翼智囊团R街研究所的刑事司法和公民自由部主任亚瑟·里泽(Arthur Rizer)说。“他们打算把钱削减到哪里？不会是从主街来的。这肯定不会是华尔街。这将是从那些提供这些人需要的东西类型的服务中获得的。“。
去年夏天，当米歇尔·罗宾逊(Michelle Robinson)出狱时，市场对劳动力如饥似渴，她的重新入狱过程相对顺利。她搬去和女儿住在一起，并在一家清理被洪水、火灾和其他自然灾害损坏的房屋的公司找到了一份工作。“在新冠肺炎开始之前，我有一份很好的工作，”她说。她在3月份被解雇，当时国家封锁了，公司的合同消失了。她每天会打开电脑三四次，申请她能找到的每一份工作，不情愿地检查方框，询问是否有重罪定罪。她很少收到回音。“泰森食品公司雇佣重罪犯，但他们已经到了极点。Servpro雇佣重罪犯，但他们已经达到最大限度。克罗格也是，但她的定罪“太新鲜”。她不能在得来速餐厅工作：“那里有抢劫案，他们不想让我数钱。”三个月后，她终于在一家餐馆找到了一份工作。
今天获释的囚犯将面临一个失业率超过11%的市场。他们将与无犯罪记录的人竞争，根据他们被监禁的时间长短，他们不会拥有现代工作场所所期望的技术技能。他们处于严重的劣势。除了这个：他们绝望了。辛格尔顿说：“我们从监狱里救出来的人会愿意在他们面临风险的地方做第一线的工作。”“他们几乎不可能比在监狱里面临更大的风险。”
实际挑战。道德问题-谁应该被释放？-更令人望而生畏。囚犯是真的忏悔，还是只是说了正确的话？他已经不再有暴力倾向了吗，还是他是一个一旦离开就会被点燃的火药桶？受害者的家人是否同意，或者他的获释只会增加他们的痛苦？难道罪行就是如此令人发指，以至于即使是完美的记录也无法克服它吗？
以纳撒尼尔·杰克逊为例，他碰巧是维罗妮卡、莎拉和克利福德的亲戚。(辛格尔顿在帮助释放安吉洛·罗宾逊时得知了纳撒尼尔·杰克逊的案件。当杰克逊的家人看到辛格尔顿如何帮助罗宾逊获释时，他们要求他调查纳撒尼尔的案件。)。1990年10月，26岁的辛辛那提毒贩杰克逊开始相信，一个名叫詹姆斯·福斯特(James Foster)的人偷了他身上的可卡因。杰克逊和他的同伙抓住福斯特，向他浇上汽油，然后放火烧了他。这名年轻人幸免于难，身体70%以上被烧伤。杰克逊和他的朋友被指控谋杀未遂，但在审判前四天，他们杀死了福斯特。杰克逊后来被判犯有严重谋杀罪，并被判处23年至终身监禁。
杰克逊现在已经在监狱里度过了28年，有些人看到的是冷酷无情的杀人犯，另一些人看到的是一个转变的人。辛格尔顿说：“他现在的任务是走出家门，防止年轻人犯他的错误。”“他现在是个好人了。他以前拉屎拉得很烂。但他完全变了。“。
辛格尔顿认为杰克逊应该得到第二次机会。但因暴力犯罪而长期服刑的囚犯几乎没有出口。没有法律邀请法官重新考虑长刑期：18个州和参议院已经提出了被称为“二次审查法案”的法案，但没有一项法案获得通过。大多数囚犯最终都会申请假释，但专家表示，假释通常会年复一年地被拒绝，通常是因为罪行的严重性，当然这一点永远不会改变。例如，杰克逊在11月被拒绝假释。州长有时会赦免囚犯，但他们不愿对像杰克逊这样的人表现出仁慈，即使他们不再对社会构成危险。尽管有致命病毒的威胁，到目前为止，州长们已经与暴力划清了界限。福特汉姆大学的约翰·普法夫说：“州长们正在做出一个非常精打细算的决定，从政治上来说，让10个人预防性地死于COVID，可能比让其中一个人在提前释放的情况下做错事要好。”“我们并不认为这些死亡事件都是有问题的。”
到目前为止，杰克逊是辛格尔顿最危险的提议。但律师认为杰克逊的监狱记录证明他值得冒这个险。他已被转移到最低安全级别的设施。根据与杰克逊共事的警卫、工作人员和志愿者的说法，杰克逊是一个“。
.
note_en:
I were to design its ideal home, it would build a prison. Inmates are packed together day and night; social distancing, frequent hand-washing, and mask wearing are fantasies. Many inmates are old, sick, and prone to infection. Most prisons are operating near capacity; some house more prisoners than they were built for. The five largest  clusters of the virus are in prisons; in Marion County, Ohio,  95 percent of inmates tested positive.
   The stark and irrefutable images of police officers killing Black men and women for small infractions have sparked protests and a larger rethinking of how America treats people who cross paths with the criminal-justice system. Even before the killing of George Floyd, the public and politicians had grown troubled by a less visible but still shocking death toll behind bars, where inmates are trapped as the virus spreads, effectively turning their prison sentences into death sentences. How do we stem the wave of infections? The answer, according to many advocates, is simple: Release prisoners.
 Some governors, alarmed at the deaths in prisons and jails and worried about the risk to surrounding communities, are listening—sort of, with an ear attuned to the political liability. More than  half of the states have agreed to release people convicted of low-level crimes, people who are nearing the end of their sentences, or people who merit compassionate release, such as pregnant people or older, vulnerable inmates.
    “It’s been helpful. I know that people have gotten out, and I am moved by their release,” says Nicole Porter, the director of advocacy at the Sentencing Project, a research organization that campaigns for sentencing reform. “But none of it has been substantial. And what I hope this moment tells us is that our incarceration rate is a function of politics—because there are many questions about who needs to be incarcerated.”
  “We’re barely scraping the surface,” says Abbe Smith, a professor at Georgetown Law School and the author of  Guilty People. “Even if we release the low-hanging fruit,” Smith says—what she calls the “non non non”: nonserious, nonviolent, nonsex offenders—“that wouldn’t make a dent.”
  To meaningfully reduce America’s prison population and slow the pandemic will require cutting away not just fat but muscle, releasing not just nonviolent drug offenders but those convicted of violent crimes. The difficulty of doing so, in both practical and moral terms, is enormous. Which people convicted of murder or armed robbery do we release? How do we decide? And how do we guarantee that they won’t offend again, especially as they try to restart their life during the worst economic collapse in nearly a century?
  In one way, the pandemic has created the perfect justification for releasing people convicted of violent crimes—stopping the virus. But at the same time, it has created the worst possible conditions for their release: They will struggle to find jobs; the organizations devoted to helping them will be strapped for funds; housing and even food may be out of reach. John Pfaff, a law professor at Fordham University and the author of  Locked In: The True Causes of Mass Incarceration and How to Achieve Real Reform, notes that in this economy, we could see an uptick in crime. “People would say: ‘Bob was released early from jail and he went off and committed this robbery,’” he says. “And the real reason for the robbery is that Bob got released early and there were no jobs; therefore, he committed the robbery. But if you’re a police chief or a [district attorney], it’s very easy to turn the economic-driven crime into criminal-justice-reform-driven crimes.” That is his nightmare, and it’s easy to see why.
  prisons are brimming with candidates who deserve a second chance—men and women who made egregious mistakes when they were young, whose crimes say more about the impulsiveness of youth and the trickiness of navigating inner-city violence than they do about character. Yet in large part, these are not people whom the system has been preparing for release.
  Prison can serve many purposes—to deter people from committing crimes in the first place, to punish them if they do, or to rehabilitate them and usher them back to normal life. America has by and large chosen the punitive path, imposing decades-long sentences intended to reduce crime on the streets. During that time, inmates usually don’t receive the kind of training or care that would enable them to return to the outside world and build a new, stable life. This presents a giant hurdle for those who would wish to release prisoners now.
    “Prison doesn’t rehabilitate you,” said Angelo Robinson, who served more than 20 years for murder. In many ways, he told me, life in prison is no different from life on the streets. “As far as the drugs, the killing, the fighting, it’s all in prison as well. The guards don’t come in there and try to help change you. No, they come there with their problems as well.” Any propulsion toward redemption comes from within. “You have to want to change your life. And a lot of guys there don’t want to.”
   Robinson’s prison story begins around 10 p.m. on February 17, 1997, as he and a few friends were doing business in an apartment in Cincinnati’s West End neighborhood. Robinson, who was 20, was locked in the bedroom, guarding the stash of crack, when he heard the front door burst open, screams, and gunfire moving down the hall toward the bedroom. “I just thought,  I’m a dead man,” Robinson recalled. He shot through the bedroom door, and when the apartment fell quiet, he found his friend, Veronica Jackson, dead in the hallway. Not until days later did he learn that it was his bullet that had killed her. “I felt horrible,” he said. “I took someone from her family. She had children. I think about it every single day.”
 Prosecutors offered him 14 years if he would agree to plead guilty to manslaughter. Robinson thought he could prove self-defense, and opted for a trial. A jury convicted him of murder and sentenced him to 29 years to life.
  Robinson’s is one story in the larger narrative of how America’s inmate population has more than quadrupled since 1981, turning prisons into crowded, unsanitary places just waiting for an infectious disease to arrive. Since the dawn of the tough-on-crime era, people convicted of violent and even some nonviolent crimes have received ever-longer sentences. This is not because Americans have become more savage; in fact, rates of violent crime have fallen by 50 percent since the early 1990s. But even as street violence has been quelled,  time served in prison for homicide and non-negligent manslaughter has tripled, and one out of  seven inmates will be in prison for life. New offenders arrive and old offenders take years to leave; prison is like a dam with no outlet, and today the reservoir is flooding its banks.
 This unforgiving policy has an emotional appeal to voters, perhaps, but defies logic based on statistics. “Why should we lock someone up for 50 years?” Pfaff asks. No other Western country does that, he says, and the social science is “unambiguously clear” that people age into and out of violent behavior. According to the Bureau of Justice Statistics,  homicide arrests peak at age 19; arrests for forcible rape peak at 18. “A 50- or 60-year-old person is just not the violent person that they were when they were 20,” Pfaff says.
    These long sentences sprang from a retributive mentality, but they also signal the smartest way to thin the ranks of prisoners during a pandemic: Start with older prisoners. “Every year we keep somebody in prison after 30, and certainly after 40 or 50, we’re getting diminishing returns for public safety,” says Marc Mauer, the executive director of the Sentencing Project. Meanwhile, Mauer says, because of poor health care, aging prisoners are growing sicker and more expensive by the year. “The costs become enormous to prevent less and less crime over time.”
 Of course, these numbers don’t account for the social cost of long sentences, the loss of a father or mother or son, the emotional strain, the dismantling of community, the hopelessness that comes when a young man is warehoused in prison for the rest of his life. For several years, Robinson marinated in that loss, until one day he thought, “ You can’t just sit here and rot.” He decided to get an education.
   Here he smacked into a roadblock to not just personal fulfillment but rehabilitation, which is, in theory at least, a cornerstone of prison. Typically, the classes, apprenticeships, and programming that might prepare an inmate to reenter society are a scarce resource given first to inmates who are within a year of release, according to experts such as Mauer. “And here I was, 29 to life,” Robinson said. “I was the last on the list.” Robinson decided to figure out another way. He snuck into classes and audited them. He managed to earn his GED, whose test is available to most prisoners. He taught himself Spanish and sign language; he learned to read music and play the guitar. He applied himself to his work, learned how to operate a forklift, to upholster furniture, to cook.
 The prioritization of inmates close to release penalizes prisoners like Robinson, who have long sentences but the possibility of parole, Mauer says. The parole board considers not just bad behavior but good, not just disciplinary actions but also classes and apprenticeships, which indicate that you want to better yourself. Someone like Robinson would come up short, “not because you don’t care, but because you haven’t had access to programs,” Mauer says. “So they say, ‘You’re denied, because you don’t have a good prison record.’”
  But Robinson was, for once, lucky. In 2012, David Singleton, the executive director of the Ohio Justice and Policy Center, represented him in a civil-rights lawsuit, forcing the prison to allow him to receive a needed surgery. “I knew this is somebody I’d love to be my neighbor one day,” Singleton says. Over the next few years, Singleton, who is also a professor at Northern Kentucky University’s Chase College of Law and a former public defender, developed a counterintuitive approach: Persuade the people who put the criminal in prison to advocate for his release. Last year, the Ohio Justice and Policy Center launched Beyond Guilt, a program that works with prosecutors and victims’ families to secure the release of those convicted of more serious crimes, including violent offenses.
    “We’re the Guilty Project,” Singleton says, laughing. “We want to represent people who are guilty but who are still human beings and deserve to not be written off.” To be considered, a prisoner must have admitted his guilt, served a significant part of his sentence, and demonstrated evidence of rehabilitation, whether formally, through classes, or reputationally, by mentoring and being a force for good in prison. The project also looks for people who have aged out of crime: those convicted in their teens and early 20s, but who have had a few years for their behavior to mellow.
   Robinson became the project’s first client. Singleton approached members of Veronica Jackson’s family about supporting his release, and all but one relative agreed. “He was a young man when this crime happened and he has served long enough,” the victim’s sister, Sarah Jackson, wrote in an affidavit. “It’s time for him to come home to his family.” The victim’s son, Clifford Jackson, had expressed  reservations in the past, telling   The New York Times in July 2019: “We were never compensated for anything. We’re only compensated by his time.” He could not be reached for comment for this story. Steve Tolbert, the chief assistant prosecutor for Hamilton County, Ohio, joined Singleton to petition the court for Robinson’s release. “Morally, it just feels right,” Tolbert told me. “This person made a huge mistake, and we’re willing to take that chance in these limited circumstances.” On August 1, 2019, Robinson pleaded guilty to manslaughter and walked out of prison. He had spent 22 years, more than half his life, behind bars, but at 43, he hoped to spend the rest of his life free.
  Last year, 15 people convicted of violent crimes—including three convicted of murder—were freed thanks to Beyond Guilt. Singleton says they are all “adjusting well to the ups and downs of life”—no small thing during a recession and a pandemic. But the story is as daunting as it is encouraging. Unless governors unilaterally release large numbers of people—an extremely unlikely prospect—these inmates will be the rarest of exceptions. How many prosecutors are willing to petition a court for the release of a criminal convicted of violence? How many victims’ families will support the freedom of a man who raped or killed or beat their sister? How many other legal organizations have the money or staff to fight this painstaking, time-consuming battle? As far as I can tell: close to zero. Last year, Beyond Guilt reduced Ohio’s 49,000-person prison population by 0.0003 percent. It’s like trying to empty the ocean with a teaspoon.
  who do make it out, building a stable life could seem all but impossible during the pandemic. It was already hard enough in normal times. Michelle Robinson knows this well.
    On the night of April 4, 2007, Robinson (no relation to Angelo) allowed two male acquaintances into her apartment so that they could rob her friend, William Means, who was spending the evening there. Means had a job and money, and during the robbery, one of the men drew a gun and shot Means to death. The prosecutor offered Robinson 10 years if she pleaded guilty to manslaughter and robbery, but on advice of her attorney, she turned it down. She was sentenced to 18 years to life. In prison, she rose to the status of an “honor lifer,” running classes for other inmates, and earning certificates in janitorial and electrical work. In 2019, Singleton and Tolbert, the prosecutor, persuaded a court to let her accept the plea she had rejected a decade earlier, and free her with time served.
 The return to civilian life, particularly for those who have spent a decade or more in prison, can be a disorienting, uphill climb. Finding an apartment, landing a job, applying for a driver’s license, paying off debts such as child-support payments when you’ve been earning 40 cents an hour—all of these make the transition a Sisyphean task. State and local governments have no obligation to help a prisoner reenter society. “They don’t have to do a thing,” Mauer notes, “and in most cases, they do a very inadequate job.”
  Some cities, such as New York, offer a range of programs—job training and placement, affordable housing, drug-treatment programs—often by funding nonprofit community or religious organizations. But if you’re unlucky enough to be from, say, rural Alabama or Mississippi—almost any rural community, in fact—you’re pretty much on your own.
  The pandemic exacerbates the problem. Could these organizations, already operating on goodwill and a shoestring, handle hundreds or thousands of people released at once? “That would stretch [them] to the limits,” Singleton says.
 “Every single state is going to go through a financial crisis,” says Arthur Rizer, the director of Criminal Justice and Civil Liberties at the R Street Institute, a center-right think tank. “Where are they going to cut their money? It’s not going to be from Main Street. It’s definitely not going to be Wall Street. It’s going to be from the services that provide the types of things that these people need to come back to the fold.”
  Last summer, when Michelle Robinson left prison, the marketplace was ravenous for labor, and her reentry proceeded relatively smoothly. She moved in with her daughter, and landed a job with a company that cleaned up houses damaged by floods, fires, and other natural disasters. “I had a good job ’til this COVID-19 started,” she says. She was laid off in March, when the state locked down and the company’s contracts disappeared. She would fire up the computer three or four times a day and apply for every job she could find, reluctantly checking the box inquiring about felony convictions. She rarely heard back. “Tyson Foods hires felons, but they’re at their max. Servpro hires felons, but they’re at their max.” So does Kroger, but her conviction is “too fresh.” She can’t work at drive-throughs: “With a robbery on there, they don’t want me counting money.” After three months, she finally landed a job at a restaurant.
    Prisoners released today will face a market with more than 11 percent unemployment. They will compete with people with clean records, and depending on how long they’ve been incarcerated, they won’t possess the technological skills expected in a modern workplace. They’re at a serious disadvantage. Except for this: They’re desperate. “The people we get out of prison will be willing to do the frontline jobs where they’re at risk,” Singleton says. “It’s almost like they can’t be at more risk than they are in prison.”
  practical challenges. The moral question—who deserves to be released?—is even more daunting. Is the inmate truly penitent, or merely saying the right words? Has he matured past his violent tendencies, or is he a tinderbox waiting to ignite once he’s out? Does the family of the victim agree, or will his release only add to their pain? Is the crime simply so heinous that even a perfect record cannot overcome it?
  Consider the case of Nathaniel Jackson, who happens to be a relative of Veronica, Sarah, and Clifford. (Singleton learned of Nathaniel Jackson’s case when he worked to help free Angelo Robinson. When Jackson's family saw how Singleton helped secure Robinson's release, they asked him to look into Nathaniel's case.) In October 1990, Jackson, a 26-year-old drug dealer in Cincinnati, came to believe that a man named James Foster had stolen cocaine from him. Jackson and his associates snatched Foster, doused him with gasoline, and set him on fire. The young man survived, with burns on more than 70 percent of his body. Jackson and his friends were charged with attempted murder, but four days before trial, they had Foster killed. Jackson was later convicted of aggravated murder and sentenced to 23 years to life.
 Jackson has now spent 28 years in prison, and where some see a callous murderer, others see a man transformed. “His mission in life now is to get out and keep young people from making his mistakes,” Singleton says. “He’s a good man now. He did bad shit before. But he’s totally changed.”
   Singleton believes that Jackson deserves a second chance. But there are few exits for prisoners serving long sentences for violent crimes. There are no laws that invite judges to reconsider long sentences: Bills that would do so, called “second-look acts,” have been introduced in 18 states and the Senate, but none has passed. Most prisoners eventually come up for parole, but experts say parole is usually denied year after year, often because of the seriousness of the crime, which of course will never change. Jackson, for example, was denied parole in November. Governors sometimes pardon inmates, but they are disinclined to show mercy toward men like Jackson, even if they no longer pose a danger to society. Even with the threat of a deadly virus, so far governors have drawn the line at violence. “Governors are making a very calculating decision that it’s probably better for them politically for 10 men to preventively die in prison from COVID than for one of them to do something wrong if he’s released early,” says Fordham’s John Pfaff. “We don’t view these deaths as all that problematic.”

title: 释放监狱人员说起来容易做起来难(2020年7月8日)
title_en: Releasing People from Prison Is Easier Said Than Done (July 8, 2020)

note:
这是关于我的两个熟人的真实故事。人们本能地知道如何在工作场所出人头地。另一个人认为他知道--而且大错特错。我们中的大多数人很可能会像后者那样行事。我相信他们的经历对我们所有人都有借鉴意义。
第一个，也就是成功的那个，我会打电话给马克。2006年春天，马克从纽约大学斯特恩商学院(Stern School Of Business)获得金融学学位，并在纽约一家小型投资公司找到了一份分析师的工作。考虑到商学院课程的残酷气氛，马克相当肯定，他进入金融职场的第一次尝试将是一次高强度、高竞争的经历。
虽然他的组织很小，但他意识到，要在队伍中晋升，他必须找到一些方法，让自己在同龄人中脱颖而出。他认为有两种传统的方法可以尝试这样做。他可以努力比他的同龄人更快、更好地完成任务，并希望因为做得更好而得到认可，或者他也可以通过找出组织中最重要的人并试图赢得他们的好感来攀登高层。
然而，他本质上并不是一个自我推销者，而且他也不确定自己在Excel电子表格方面是否能超越其他人。他决定，在决定选修哪门课程之前，他需要尽可能多地了解他所工作的公司。
从第一天开始，马克就会问人们一些问题，比如他们在做什么，他们和谁一起工作，以及他们是如何完成工作的。不管一个人是初级的还是高级的、行政助理还是首席投资者，这都无关紧要。他只是想知道他能做些什么，了解他们做了什么，以及他为之工作的组织。
一旦他对公司内部的所有活动部件都有了清晰的认识，他就开始寻找可以改善公司运营的方法。进行这些改进超出了他的工作范围，但他认为，利用他对组织中的人如何运作的理解，修复他可以轻松修复的东西是有意义的。
起初，马克所做的改进一点也不吸引人。事实上，他的同龄人嘲笑他把时间浪费在他们说永远不会增加他奖金的行为上。他开始为这家投资集团的每周例会订购午餐，并确保按时订购合适数量的办公用品。很明显，他并不介意在需要帮助的地方出手相助，他的上级也开始注意到他的职业道德。他们看到他确保人们获得高效完成工作所需的工具。他们看到每个人都从中受益。
马克既不是天生的网络工作者，也不是竞争对手，他已经开始有机地建立网络。人们欣赏他的所作所为，因为这不是建立在自我推销的基础上，也是因为这真的帮助了他们。
最终，随着Mark更多地了解组织的需求，他意识到如果使用新的工具和技能来完成某些任务，需要进行的一些更改会更容易。没有一个让团队失望的人，他开始在晚上自学新的Excel函数和其他软件程序。很快，他就成了Excel方面的专家，这是他的投资团队中的首选人员，负责让他的同行加快掌握新技术。实际上，他是在设法做到这一点。
作为公司中极少数充分了解内部管理需求和外部投资者要求的人之一，他开始参加有关合规、新软件和简化流程以提高组织整体效率的战略会议。因此，一个不自我推销、不参与竞争的新手发现自己在管理和训练他的同龄人。凭借新获得的技能和专业知识，他的角色表现超出了预期，并被公认为组织内的战略思想家和领导者。在第一年结束时，他被提升为高级分析师，并获得了比任何同龄人都高出50%的奖金。
马克通过寻求了解和协助整个组织，而不是通过直接竞争以牺牲他人的利益来提升自己，从而实现了所有这一切。他帮助每个人更好地完成自己的工作，从而成为一名天生的促进者、专家，并最终成为领导者。
与此同时，马克的同事泰德--我也改了他的名字--走了一条不同的、更传统的道路。他疯狂地工作，试图证明自己比马克和所有其他分析师都要好。
刚开始的时候，他不确定其他分析师有多有才华，但他认为，如果他在办公室呆得晚一些，少花点时间在吃午饭之类的无关紧要的事情上，他可能至少能比他们中的大多数人做得更好。他密切关注着他们在做什么(除了那个浪费时间订午餐的笨蛋马克)，并确保注意到他如何才能证明自己有理由接管他们的一些工作。
他积极地与人交往。他顺道拜访了办公室的高级管理人员，表达了他渴望承担更多工作的愿望。他一定要提到他已经完成的任务，并让他们知道他在大学里学过的相关课程，这些课程可能会让他有资格承担额外的责任。
泰德不知道--也不关心--投资团队以外的任何人都做了些什么。高级经理是要给人留下深刻印象的人，而他的分析师同行是要保持领先的人。他有时很难得到管理团队的帮助来完成交易，但他没有让这阻止他。事实上，他经常会在打断高级经理的时候--呃，会议--提到他对行政人员的失望。
奖金季节即将来临时，泰德确信自己会成为第一个被提拔的分析师。毕竟，他做事情做得最快，与高级管理人员的关系也最密切。令他震惊和失望的是，他没有得到第一次晋升。他拿到了奖金，但并不比大多数其他分析师多。发生了什么事？他们有没有设法跟他一样快？
泰德没有意识到的是，每个被聘为分析师的人都是才华横溢、才华横溢的。他们都把工作做完了，而且都做得很好。当然，更努力、更快的工作让他引起了人们的注意，但这只是为了做更多同样的事情。
虽然泰德正在学习更快地完成他的工作，但他并没有学习做其他任何事情。他在任何时候都没有协助、管理或领导--可以推荐他升职的活动。更重要的是，他一直在要求他的经理们承担更多责任，而不是有机地承担责任，并表明他可以处理这些责任。
在课堂上，他的方法会很管用。他不会打断管理层，而是在办公时间定期拜访教授。他把注意力放在分配给他的任务上，这会使他成为班上成绩最好的明星学生。任务完成。
另一方面，在工作中，泰德在他所做的事情上仍然是表现最好的，但他是轮子上的仓鼠，试图保持领先于所有其他聪明能干的员工。更糟糕的是，他总是担心新的竞争对手。他陷入了无休止的压力循环之中。
让我们来看看马克到底做对了什么，这让他有别于泰德--也有别于公司其他所有刚开始工作的人。
1.了解事物的工作原理。他开始工作的第一步就是尽可能多地了解他所工作的组织。他更多的是出于好奇心和想要理解自己陷入了什么境地的愿望，而不是想要超越同龄人的雄心。因此，他很快就了解了人们和他们的角色，并没有表现出任何他只是想要推销自己的感觉。
2.了解每个人都在做什么，以及他们是如何做的。通过询问他人而不是推销自己，马克比一些高级管理层成员更了解该组织。结果，他成了想出最好的办法把事情做好的人。
注意：当你对与上级交谈感到不自在时，你真的不知道，一封简单的电子邮件就可以做到这一点。自我介绍一下，让对方知道你是新来的，正在努力全面了解公司，你只想对每个人的工作做一两句简短的介绍。这可能在中小型组织中效果最好。在较大的组织中，公司内部网通常可以帮助您处理事情，尽管它们在纸面上的工作方式和在实践中的工作方式有时可能会非常不同。在最小的组织中，简单的观察通常就足以了解谁做了什么以及如何做。
3.了解哪里存在差距，并向他人传达如何填补这些差距。在马克的同龄人群体中，除了他们自己的职责之外，没有其他人抽出时间来深入了解这家公司。他们忙于竞争(在某些情况下还闲聊)。马克了解每个人是如何完成工作的，他能够在会议上根据他自己能够提出的观察结果提出建议。他不是通灵师，他只是在集中注意力。
4.找出组织问题的解决方案，快速解决问题。由于了解事情的实际运作方式，马克能够发现问题并提出解决方案。大多数人甚至不知道这些问题就是问题。他们忙于自己的角色，没有注意到这一点。马克提出解决方案的能力给了他战略思想家的优势，因为他做出了快速、容易的改变，这些改变对他作为观察者来说是显而易见的，但对那些在具体职责中迷失的人来说往往不是那么明显。
5.不怕枯燥乏味的工作，需要帮助的地方就出手相助。马克的成功之路始于谦虚地点午餐。但这给了他每周花几分钟了解每个人的日程安排并进行交谈的机会。当然，同事们的言论一开始让他担心自己会被归类为吃午饭的人，但他每周有目的地与高级管理层接触，让他有机会提到自己对最新金融新闻的任何想法。无论如何，订午餐只是马克解决问题议程上的众多项目之一。这只花了几分钟，所以这并没有妨碍他从事其他工作；最终很容易委派给其他人(让他看起来更像是一名经理)；这让他变得脚踏实地、深思熟虑，同时也很聪明，这让他在各个层面都很受欢迎。
6.为自己和他人确定联系。了解组织内部工作的一个好处是您可以看到各个部分是如何交互的。一旦你看到了这一点，你就准备好了促进跨职能和跨团队的互动--你就拥有了一个重要的战略家和领导者的工具，他必须在做出有效和适当的决策之前考虑到形势的整体情况。此外，了解影响你工作职能的联系会让你更有生产力和效率，而不会真正更加努力地工作。
马克对他的工作采取了某种偶然的方式，这并不是实现职业晋升的唯一途径，但它确实驳斥了这样的假设，即最好的或唯一的前进道路是我们大多数人从一年级开始就一直追求的道路。
Avril David是全球管理咨询公司Project Performance Corporation的能源和环境分析师，也是与职业、能源、气候政策和绿色商业相关主题的自由撰稿人。
note_en:
This is a true story about two acquaintances of mine. One knew instinctively exactly how to get ahead in the workplace. The other thought he knew--and was dead wrong. Most of us would probably behave pretty much the way the latter did. I believe their experiences hold lessons for all of us.
 The first of them, the successful one, I'll call Mark. Mark got a degree in finance from New York University's Stern School of Business in the spring of 2006 and landed a job as an analyst at a small investment firm in New York. Given the cutthroat atmosphere of his business school classes, Mark was fairly certain that his first foray into the working world of finance would be a high intensity, high-competition experience.
 Though his organization was small, he realized that to rise within the ranks he would have to find some way to differentiate himself among his peers. He figured there were two traditional ways he could try to do so. He could strive to perform his tasks faster and better than his peers and hope to be recognized for doing a better job, or he could schmooze his way to the top by identifying the most important people in the organization and trying to win their favor.
 However, he wasn't a self-promoter by nature, and he also wasn't sure he could outpace other people at the kind of work in Excel spreadsheets he'd be doing. He decided that before deciding which course to take, he'd need to learn all he could about the company he was working for.
 From day one, Mark asked people questions about what they were working on, who they were working with and how they got their work done. It didn't matter if a person was junior or senior, administrative assistant or lead investor. He simply wanted to know what he could about what they did and the organization he was working for.
 Once he had a clear sense of all of the moving parts within the company, he began to see ways its operations could be improved. Making those improvements lay outside his job description, but he believed it made sense to fix what he could easily fix, drawing on the understanding he was gathering of how the people in the organization operated.
 At first the improvements Mark made were far from glamorous. In fact his peers derided him for wasting his time on actions they said would never increase his bonus. He began ordering lunch for the investment group's weekly meeting and making sure office supplies were ordered on time and in the right quantities. It was obvious he didn't mind pitching in where help was needed, and his supervisors began to notice his work ethic. They saw him making sure that people got the tools that they needed to do their jobs efficiently. And they saw everyone benefiting.
 Without being a natural networker and without competing, Mark had begun networking organically. People appreciated what he did because it wasn't based on self-promotion and because it genuinely helped them.
 Eventually, as Mark learned more about the needs of the organization, he realized that some of the changes that needed to be made would be easier if new tools and skills were used to complete certain tasks. Not one to let down the team, he began teaching himself new Excel functions and other software programs in the evenings. Soon he was an expert at Excel, the go-to person in his investment group and responsible for getting his peers up to speed on new techniques. In effect he was managing.
 As one of very few people at the company who fully understood both internal administrative needs and external investor requirements, he began to be included in strategic meetings regarding compliance, new software and the streamlining of processes to make the organization as a whole more effective. And so a non-self-promoting, non-competing newbie found himself managing and training his peers. He was exceeding performance expectations for his role with the newly acquired skills and expertise and was being recognized as a strategic thinker and leader within the organization. He was promoted to senior analyst by the end of his first year and received a bonus 50% bigger than any of his peers got.
 Mark achieved all this by seeking to know and assist the organization as a whole rather than by directly competing to promote himself at the expense of others. He helped everyone do their jobs better and thereby became a natural facilitator, expert and, finally, leader.
 Meanwhile, Mark's co-worker Ted--whose name I have also changed--took a different, more traditional path. He worked like a maniac to try to show that he was better than Mark and all the other analysts.
 When he started, he wasn't sure how talented the other analysts were, but he figured that if he stayed in the office later and spent less time on unimportant things like eating lunch, he would probably be able to do a better job than at least most of them. He kept an eye on what they worked on (except for that dunce Mark, who wasted time ordering lunches) and made sure to take note of how he could make a case for taking over some of their work.
 He networked aggressively. He dropped in to see members of senior management in their offices to express his eagerness to take on more work. He made sure to mention tasks he had already completed and to let them know of relevant courses he had taken in college that likely qualified him for added responsibility.
 Ted didn't know--or care--what anyone outside the investment team did. The senior managers were the people to impress, and his fellow analysts were the people to keep ahead of. He sometimes had a hard time getting the administrative team's help in closing trades, but he didn't let that stop him. In fact, he'd often mention his disappointment with administrative staffers at his interruptions--er, meetings--with senior managers.
 By the time bonus season rolled around, Ted felt sure he'd be the first analyst promoted. After all, he was the fastest at what he did and had the closest relationships with senior managers. To his shock and disappointment, he was passed over for that first promotion. He received a bonus, but he got no more than most of the other analysts. What had happened? Had they somehow managed to be just as fast as him?
 What Ted had failed to realize was that everyone hired as an analyst was talented and bright. They all got their jobs done, and they all did them very well. Sure, working harder and faster got him noticed, but only for doing more of the same.
 Although Ted was learning to do his job more speedily, he wasn't learning to do anything else. At no point was he facilitating, managing or leading--activities that could recommend him for advancement. More important, he had been asking his managers for more responsibility rather than taking on responsibilities organically and showing that he could handle them.
 In the classroom his approach would have worked well. Instead of interrupting management, he would have been regularly visiting professors during office hours. His focus on his assigned tasks above all else would have made him a star student with the best grades in the class. Mission accomplished.
 At work, on the other hand, Ted was still a top performer at what he did, but he was a hamster on a wheel trying to stay ahead of all the other bright and capable employees. Even worse, he was always worried about new competition. He was caught in an unending cycle of stress.
 Let's examine what Mark did right that set him apart from Ted--and from everyone else starting out at the company.
 1. Understanding how things work. His first move when he began his job was to learn as much as he could about the organization he was working for. He was driven more by curiosity and a desire to comprehend what he had gotten himself into than by ambition to outperform his peers. As a result, he quickly got to know people and their roles, without conveying any sense that he was just trying to promote himself.
 2. Knowing what everyone does and how they do it. By asking questions about others rather than selling himself, Mark came to know more about the organization than some members of senior management. As a result, he became a go-to person for figuring out the best ways to get things done.
 Note: When you're not comfortable speaking with a higher-up you don't really know, a simple e-mail can do the trick. Introduce yourself and let the person know that you're new and trying to get a full grasp of the organization, and you'd just like a quick sentence or two about what each person does. This is likely to work best at small to medium-size organizations. At larger organizations, the company Intranet can often help you get a handle on things, though how they work on paper and how they work in practice can sometimes be very different. At the smallest organizations, simple observation is often enough for learning who does what and how.
 3. Learning where gaps exist and conveying to others how to fill them. No one else in Mark's peer group took the time to learn much about the company beyond their own responsibilities. They were too busy competing (and in some cases schmoozing). Mark, having a sense of how everyone got their jobs done, he was able to make recommendations at meetings based on observations that he alone had been able to come up with. He wasn't psychic; he was just paying attention.
 4. Identifying solutions to organizational problems and making quick fixes. Being privy to how things actually worked, Mark was able to identify problems and propose solutions. Most people had no idea that the problems even were problems. They were too busy within their own roles to notice. Mark's ability to propose solutions gave him an edge as a strategic thinker as he made quick, easy changes that were obvious to him as an observer but often not so obvious to those lost in their specific duties.
 5. Being unafraid of unglamorous work, and pitching in where help is needed. Mark's path to success began with humbly ordering lunches. But that gave him a chance to spend a few minutes each week getting a sense of everyone's schedules and making conversation. Sure, remarks by his co-workers made him fear at first that he'd get pigeon-holed as the lunch guy, but his purposeful weekly access to senior management gave him a moment to mention any thoughts he had on the latest financial news. And anyway, ordering lunches was just one of many items on Mark's problem-solving agenda. It took only a few minutes, so it didn't keep him from his other work; it was easy to eventually delegate to someone else (making him look like more of a manager); and it established him as down-to-earth and thoughtful as well as bright, making him well-liked at all levels.
 6. Identifying linkages, for himself and others. One benefit of knowing the inner workings of an organization is that you can see how the parts interact. Once you see that, you're equipped to facilitate interactions across functions and groups--and you've got an important tool of a strategizer and leader, who has to absorb the whole picture in a situation before he can make effective and appropriate decisions. Furthermore, understanding the linkages that affect your job function makes you more productive and effective without actually working any harder.
 The somewhat accidental approach Mark took to his job is hardly the only way to achieve career advancement, but it does give the lie to the assumption that the best or only way ahead is the one most of us have pursued ever since the first grade.
 Avril David is an energy and environment analyst for Project Performance Corporation, a global management consulting firm, and a freelance writer on topics related to careers, energy, climate policy and green business.
title: “如何在工作中快速崛起：真实故事”(2009)
title_en: How to Rise Fast at Work: A True Story (2009)

note:
谷歌今天发布了一篇博客文章，详细介绍了其在改善Android生态系统更新速度方面的进展。在过去的几年里，该公司一直在努力将Android模块化，希望让Android更容易升级，从而导致设备制造商更快地推出更新。谷歌的努力得到了回报，该公司宣布，Android 10的推出速度是有史以来最快的。
最近几个版本的Android都对Android的更新系统进行了重大改进。Android 8引入了Project Treble，它将操作系统与硬件支持分离，使得Android可以更轻松地跨设备移植。在Android 9Pie中，谷歌完成了对Treble的开发，并开始发布通用系统映像(GSI)：Android的临时版本，可以在任何与Project Treble兼容的设备上运行。Android 10引入了Project Mainline和新的APEX文件类型，这些文件类型是为可更新的低级系统组件设计的，通过Play Store交付。
谷歌的统计数据显示，所有这些工作实际上都在改善生态系统。谷歌写道，多亏了这些努力，Android 10的普及速度比以往任何版本的Android都要快。发布5个月后，Android 10在1亿台设备上运行--比Android Pie快28%。
在图表的末尾，Android 10的用户达到了4亿，达到了300天大关(Android 10已经诞生了310天)，这听起来是一个相当大的数字。不过，谷歌表示，截至去年，目前有25亿台活跃的Android设备，因此4亿用户相当于活跃Android安装基数的16%。从奥利奥(Oreo)到派(Pie)再到安卓10(Android 10)，使用率都在增加，这很好，但仍有很大的改进空间。
Android更新的下一个大前沿是一个更便携的Linux内核，而不是今天每个Android设备都独一无二地开发的严重分叉的、特定于设备的Linux内核。我们上一次写这个项目是在2019年末，当时我们第一次讨论了为Android构建通用内核映像(Generic Kernel Image，GKI)的计划。我们的目标是一种Linux内核的三元组项目。可以使用可移植的通用内核映像，并且专有的驱动程序和代码可以作为内核模块加载，两个代码库之间有一个稳定的接口，而不是现在存在的愉快的进程。这一点还与发布主线Linux内核(而不是特定于Android的Linux)联系在一起讨论，这意味着您可以在Linux桌面上运行Android的相同内核。
今天，谷歌宣布，在Android 11中，GKI系统将启动并运行。谷歌表示，在Android 11中，我们将进一步隔离Android Linux内核中的公共代码，以创建可在所有Android设备上运行的通用内核映像(GKI)，并实现更快的安全部署。
Android Linux内核具体指的不是主流Linux内核，所以这仍然是一个分叉，但它将内核集成到Android版本中以及进行次要的安全版本更新现在应该更容易了。如今，Linux内核在面世之前要经过三次分叉：LTS版本通过Android操作系统特定的更改由谷歌分叉为Android通用内核，然后分叉为SoC专用内核(通常由高通提供)，然后由设备制造商分叉为设备专用内核。GKI将采用Android Common内核-第一个分支-并在设备上运行它。所以它仍然是叉子，但它不再是叉子了。
XDA的米沙尔·拉赫曼(Mishaal Rahman)表示，他看到了尚未发布的Android 11版本的Android兼容性定义文件(CDD)，所有制造商都必须遵守这些规则才能获得谷歌应用的授权。他说，就像通用系统映像一样，运行通用内核映像将是谷歌认证过程的一部分。所有的Google Play设备都不需要证明它们可以通过谷歌的兼容性测试套件，而且操作系统和内核都是原封不动的。想必，就像今天通用系统映像的情况一样，制造商不会指望随附这个通用代码，它只会被用作手机附带的驱动程序和其他专有代码的基准。想必，就像今天的GSI一样，Modelers将会因通用Android内核而大发横财。
谷歌说在接下来的几个月里，请继续关注GKI上更详细的帖子。
title: Android 10的更新率是有史以来最快的，10个月内就达到了16%的用户
title_en: Android 10 has the fastest update rate ever, hits 16% of users in 10 months
